articles.title: The Thrift Economy
articles.subtitle: How Indian GenZ Rewired Fashion from the Ground Up
articles.author: Arjun Mehta
articles.lead: From Sarojini Nagar hauls to curated Instagram drops, a generation built an entire aesthetic universe on the rejection of the mainstream retail complex.

body.text: Walk through Sarojini Nagar on a Saturday morning and you will not find tourists. You will find 22-year-olds with phones clamped between their shoulders, FaceTiming their friends in Pune about a pair of cargo pants. You will find people who understand, at a gut level, that fashion is not what you buy — it's what you know.

This is the thrift economy. It runs on information asymmetry, cultural fluency, and a deep distrust of anything that asks too much for too little. It is the dominant mode of style for Indian GenZ, and it has produced some of the most interesting fashion happening anywhere in the world right now.
We're not anti-consumption. We're anti-stupidity. Why pay 8,000 rupees for a fast-fashion brand when you can find something better for 400?

The thrift economy didn't emerge from ideology. It emerged from a generation that had less money than the generations before it, more visual sophistication, and social media platforms that made global fashion immediately visible and legible. When you can see what Depop looks like in London — then look at your local budget — you either check out or get creative. Indian GenZ got creative.

The critical infrastructure of this economy is the resale account. Thousands exist on Instagram, each operating as a micro-boutique. The best ones have waitlists. Some have turned into actual businesses with turnovers that would embarrass a lot of 'legitimate' fashion labels.

articles.title: Floors Without Walls
articles.subtitle: India's Rave Diaspora
articles.author: Priya Nair
articles.lead: From Anjuna Beach parties to underground Bombay techno — the full, strange lineage of India's dancefloor underground.

body.text: Before it was a brand — before it was a genre name, a package holiday, a Spotify playlist — Goa trance was a place. It was the Anjuna flea market car park at 3am. Travellers who had come to Goa and, somewhere in the process of staying too long, invented something new.
The Indian rave scene had to quietly grow up in Goa trance's shadow before it could claim its own sound.
The scene is simultaneously more visible and more underground than ever. The energy that matters is still in WhatsApp groups with location pins that change three times before the night is over.

articles.title: No Logo Required
articles.subtitle: Homegrown Labels Winning Without Hype
articles.author: Kabir Singh
articles.lead: Five Indian labels doing it independently — no VC money, no celebrity co-signs, no compromises.

body.text: There is a version of Indian homegrown fashion that is very interested in being noticed. And then there are the labels that are not doing any of that. The ones where you find out about them because someone whose taste you trust is wearing something you cannot place.
We don't do PR. We don't do influencer seeding. Our entire marketing budget is the product itself.

articles.title: The Psy-Trance Revival
articles.subtitle: Why Gen Z Inherited It
articles.author: Ananya Rao
articles.lead: Why the sound of Goa in the 90s is the sound of India's warehouses in 2026.

body.text: Psy-trance has been declared dead more times than most genres have been declared alive. A genre that has never been cool enough to become uncool occupies a very specific kind of freedom.
My parents went to Goa trance parties in the 90s. I play psy-trance sets in Delhi basements. Same sound. Completely different.

articles.title: Dressing Against the Grain
articles.subtitle: Queer Fashion in Urban India
articles.author: Siya Kapoor
articles.lead: How queer communities are shaping the most radical edge of Indian street fashion — in full view.

body.text: To dress queerly in urban India in 2026 is to make an argument. Not the old argument of survival — but a louder argument about what the street looks like when you actually look at it.
I'm not dressing to be understood. I'm dressing to be seen. Those are different things.

brands.name: KAASAW
brands.desc: Deconstructed silhouettes rooted in Dalit Bahujan identity. Handwoven cotton, raw edges, no apologies.
brands.story: KAASAW began in Dharavi in 2022 when founder Rohini Kamble started reworking surplus fabric into pieces that reflected a Dalit Bahujan identity the mainstream fashion industry had never thought to represent. The name means crow — intelligence, memory, survival.

brands.name: OBLIQUE
brands.desc: Streetwear that takes from temple architecture and returns it as bombers and wide-leg cuts.
brands.story: Founder Dev Sharma spent three years studying Hoysala temple architecture before making his first garment. The rhythm of columns, the logic of proportion — these become the grammar of pattern-cutting.

brands.name: MURMUR
brands.desc: Surplus fabric sourced from garment factories. Remixed into something new.
brands.story: MURMUR was founded on a simple premise: Delhi's garment industry produces more fabric than it uses. The constraint of found material produced better design decisions than a blank canvas.

brands.name: NOIR DUST
brands.desc: Rave-ready clubwear that works on a beach at 4am.
brands.story: NOIR DUST was designed for a specific environment: the outdoor party at the end of a long night. The clothes are built around that constraint with unusual precision.

brands.name: BHUVI
brands.desc: Traditional craft techniques — block print, resist dye — worn differently.
brands.story: BHUVI works directly with block printing and resist dye artisans in Rajasthan. Founder Meera Sharma created a market for craft practiced for generations.

brands.name: WILT
brands.desc: Slow fashion. Distressed textures, earth tones.
brands.story: WILT makes clothes meant to degrade intentionally — through a design philosophy that treats the garment's lifespan as part of its aesthetic.

brands.name: NEEDLE
brands.desc: Every piece numbered. Every season a limited print run.
brands.story: NEEDLE treats each season as a zine: finite print run, a specific number, a collector's logic applied to clothing. When the run is sold, it's gone.

brands.name: FLORA
brands.story: FLORA is inseparable from Kolkata — its humidity, its faded grandeur, its specific relationship with colonial history. Every piece processes something.
